comments.text: How do they treat Muslims ?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Norway has a small but established Muslim population, mostly from immigration over the past few decades, with legal protections for religious freedom same as any other faith. Like most of Europe, it's a topic with real social debate around integration, but there's no different legal standing for Muslims specifically.

comments.text: I have been to Norway. It is a beautiful country. I have no idea why people claim that Norway is the wealthiest country in the world. Think about it. Use your reasoning. I am sure Norway is better off with its offshore oil reserve than not having any oil reserve. For that I am sure Norwegian oil companies will make money and the people of Norway will have good paying jobs. As for me Norway's greatest asset is its beautiful landscape.

comments.author: @southbirdsouthbird
comments.text: As a Norwegian-American, I can totally relate to this wonderful video.  My Norwegian ancestors first came to the USA in 1848 from the western fjord country (Sognefjordane) during the potatoe famine (yes, it hit Norway as well as Ireland).  They struggled and suffered incredibly (I will spare you the details) but survived and prospered and taught me and my generation all the same lessons you have highlighted.  All my large Norwegian extended family (counting now in the hundreds or even thousands) live by the same precepts:  Be humble, support your community, help your neighbor, get moving, get outside and get to work, don't show off, etc.  Now that I am old and accomplished and relatively wealthy I take comfort in those inherited lessons:   I live simply and frugally,  even though I don't have to.  I give away much of my wealth to deserving causes.  And from that I feel great satisfaction.  When I visit Norway I feel totally at home and at peace with the people and the landscape.  God Bless the people of Norway!  Thank you for showing the rest of us the way to live!

comments.author: @aldyendonnelly1033
comments.text: Norwegian families carry the highest private debt--at 236% of disposable household income--in the world. Norway's wealthiest have been leaving, lately. That is why Norway has an "exit tax". Not sure that this promo piece adequately addresses these realities.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair points to raise — household debt and capital flight are real pressures underneath the surface story we told. We focused on the cultural side of wealth rather than the full economic picture, but appreciate you adding the nuance here.

comments.author: @SleepyCritter1
comments.text: If Norway is really this rich, can anyone who immigrates there share in its vast $2T fund?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Not directly — the fund doesn't pay out to individuals, citizens or immigrants alike. Its returns flow into the national budget and help fund public services like healthcare and education, so the benefit is more indirect than a personal share.

comments.author: @Lars-q3c
comments.text:
Thank you for presenting Norway to the world!
However, I feel you put to much weight on the wealth fund.
Norway was not a poor country before we found oil: we had a balanced inland economy with imports and exports, industry, shipping, and a social security among the best at the time oil was found. We had rebuilt after the war and had a healthy consumption. Yes, the oil helped and changed us, but as you say it didn't define us.
Moreover, we have been living in this place for 10.000 years and there is plenty of history you could have told.
How people survived along our long coastline thousands of years ago, how we through the viking age made democracy, how we traded, how Christianity came to us.  You mentioned the Hanseatic period, but nothing about Norwegian shipbuilding, shipping or timber exports. Nothing about our national romantic period, how we had the animals on summer pastures in the Highlands. I heard nothing about the Sami and kveen people, our political system, or that we now are a society with high-tech production. We are neighbors with the Swedes the Danes and the Finnish and how this has affected us. Our claim to the south pole, our administration of Svalbard and our relationship with the Russians.
I know there is only so much you can talk about in an hour, but these are topics who made us who we are, the money is very recent and can through a global recession be gone tomorrow.

Then again, maybe I misunderstood the point of this video. Maybe it is the point to show how wealth didn't change us, more than showing Norway and it's history;)

However, thank you for what you did!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: This is exactly the kind of detailed feedback we want — thank you for taking the time. You're right that an hour forces hard choices, and the pre-oil history, the Sami and Kven peoples, shipbuilding, and the Arctic relationships you mention are all real gaps worth addressing in future coverage.

comments.text:
As a Canadian born on the island of Newfoundland…the eastern most province of the country…I was fortunate to spend a few childhood years in a town that has the colourful, identical houses seen in the coastal communities of Norway.  If I had to live outside beautiful British Columbia - I would certainly consider Norway and the other Nordic countries, as it is very similar culturally and ethically in many ways.

Thankfully, Canada is following Norway when it comes to the Sovereign Fund route…and we are now finally growing closer to our European “cousins” via free trade in resources, agriculture, military, tech, critical minerals, etc.  The closer our ties are - the better our futures will become…but…we must maintain the ethics, humility and morality of those wiser than us, including generations who came before, so we may have a future!

comments.author: @Serpentbane69
comments.text:
Even though oil became incredibly important for Norway, it’s a common misconception that Norway “had nothing” before oil was discovered in 1969. Norway already had several strong economic pillars long before the first drop of offshore oil was pumped.

- Hydropower was basically Norway’s first “oil.” Cheap, abundant electricity built our early industry.
- Shipping made Norway one of the world’s biggest maritime nations.
- Fisheries were a major export sector for generations.
- Forestry, mining, and metal production were also key parts of the economy.

Norway also had established a relatively high level literacy at the time with a strong public school system.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Every single point here adds a layer the oil story tends to flatten. The maritime chapter alone could carry its own film. Thank you for that. 🌿

comments.author: @brkrå21ø5gxdyn1æ
comments.text:
As a foreigner who has been here for a number of years, the title is quite misleading.

People do show off here but in a much more subdued way. You will only see it if you look hard enough.

1) Constantly doing exterior and interior home renovations, just because.. Back when I was new here, shabby chic was all the rage- everything from paints, textiles, ceramics, furnitures. Even the tablescaping on important days like Constitution Day, Easter or Christmas stayed true to that design era. Then it was the grey era, greige era, Japandi. The current trend leans more towards colors, some brave ones have become maximalists.

Norway is actually one of the biggest spenders on interior design and home improvement.

2) How and where they spend their holidays and free time. Winter ski trips up on the mountain, spent on their "hut" with all of the modern conveniences you can find. When summer rolls around they go on discovering islets and skerries to get their summer tan. Then they travel abroad too, some more than twice per year.

Summer vacations are actually a heated political discussion here. Coming from Asia, it was jarring to hear this at first. As per the news, some schoolchildren are bullied because they spend their holidays at home-- no mountain hut, no summer boat, no international trip.

3) The old generation might be adherrents to the Janteloven, but this is getting less and less practised by the Tiktok generation. Teenagers here wear expensive winter jackets or backpacks. The girls splurge on luxury bags and or makeup. Just last Christmas on my local newspaper, a mother received heavy criticism for wanting a Ralph Lauren pullover for her child on the Wishing Tree.

My province is a favorite playground of the rich where they spend their Norwegian summers. Conflicts between them and the local authorities /local residents are aplenty when it comes to building approvals. The absence of megamansions might be attributed to local laws and or pushback from local residents. This doesn't mean the rich are not pushing the boundaries, though!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: These details — the renovation cycles, the hut culture, the vacation politics — are exactly what a 90-minute window can't hold. The title is aimed at the world looking in; what you're describing is the quiet competition that runs inside it. Did the holiday pressure surprise you most, or did something else catch you off-guard when you first arrived?

comments.author: @biber9979
comments.text: Norway goes too far with savings. Too much is too much. They are by far richest country in Europe but when it's time to pay their citizens good salary they aren't there. In one of the most expensive countries of the world university graduates who work as kindergarten teachers make less than 3000 euros or little more than that after few years. Child benefit is also just 200 euros and that is less than in Austria, Germany... country should get a high speed rail line to connect Oslo-Stavanger-Bergen but no they don't want to spend money on infrastructure...while countries with way less money are doing that....Portugal, Turkey, Serbia...

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That gap between the headline wealth and the everyday wage is exactly the tension the film could only gesture at. The kindergarten teacher figure especially — thank you for adding that layer.
